event_text: William, Duke of Normandy defeats Harold Godwinson at the Battle of Hastings, beginning Norman rule in England.
William I is crowned King of England at Westminster Abbey.
The Domesday survey is completed in 1086, recording landholdings and resources across much of England.
Henry I issues the Charter of Liberties after becoming king.
The White Ship sinks, killing William Adelin and creating a succession crisis.
Stephen is crowned king, leading into the civil conflict later known as The Anarchy.
Henry II becomes king and begins the Plantagenet dynasty in England.
The Constitutions of Clarendon attempt to define royal authority over the English church.
Thomas Becket is murdered in Canterbury Cathedral.
Richard I becomes king of England.
John becomes king after the death of Richard I.
King John agrees to Magna Carta at Runnymede, limiting royal power and asserting legal rights.
The Treaty of Lambeth helps end the First Barons War and confirms the settlement under Henry III.
The Provisions of Oxford impose a council-led reform programme on Henry III.
Simon de Montfort defeats royal forces at Lewes and becomes a leading political power.
Simon de Montfort calls a parliament including knights and burgesses.
Edward I becomes king and later strengthens royal administration.
Edward I expels Jews from England by the Edict of Expulsion.
The Model Parliament is summoned, influencing later parliamentary development.
Edward II becomes king of England.
English forces are defeated by the Scots at Bannockburn.
Edward II is forced to abdicate and Edward III becomes king.
The Hundred Years War begins between England and France.
The Black Death reaches England and causes widespread population loss.
The Statute of Labourers attempts to control wages after the Black Death.
English becomes the language of courts under the Pleading in English Act.
The Peasants Revolt begins in Essex and Kent, challenging taxation and social conditions.
Henry Bolingbroke deposes Richard II and becomes Henry IV.
Henry V wins the Battle of Agincourt during the Hundred Years War.
Henry VI becomes king as an infant.
The First Battle of St Albans opens the Wars of the Roses between the houses of Lancaster and York.
Henry Tudor defeats Richard III at Bosworth and becomes Henry VII.
The Battle of Stoke Field ends the main fighting of the Wars of the Roses.
Henry VIII becomes king of England.
The Act of Supremacy makes Henry VIII supreme head of the Church of England.
The Dissolution of the Monasteries transforms religious and social life in England.
The first Act of Union with Wales brings Wales into the English legal system.
Edward VI becomes king and Protestant reforms accelerate.
Mary I becomes queen and restores Roman Catholic worship.
Elizabeth I becomes queen, beginning the Elizabethan era.
William Shakespeare is baptised at Stratford-upon-Avon.
Mary, Queen of Scots is executed at Fotheringhay Castle.
The English fleet attacks the Spanish Armada near Gravelines, helping defeat the invasion attempt.
James VI of Scotland becomes James I of England, uniting the crowns.
The Gunpowder Plot against James I is discovered.
The King James Bible is published.
The Mayflower sails from Plymouth with English separatists bound for North America.
Charles I becomes king of England.
Parliament presents the Petition of Right to Charles I.
Charles I raises his standard at Nottingham, marking the start of the English Civil War.
Charles I is executed and England becomes a commonwealth.
Oliver Cromwell becomes Lord Protector.
The monarchy is restored and Charles II becomes king.
Deaths from the Great Plague in London rise sharply during the summer of 1665.
The Great Fire of London destroys large parts of the city.
William of Orange lands at Brixham, a decisive moment in the Glorious Revolution.
The English Bill of Rights limits royal power and confirms parliamentary rights.
The Acts of Union join England and Scotland as the Kingdom of Great Britain.
George I becomes king, beginning the Hanoverian succession.
Robert Walpole becomes the leading minister and is often regarded as Britain’s first prime minister.
The British Museum opens to the public in London.
George III becomes king.
Richard Arkwright opens Cromford Mill, a key early factory of the Industrial Revolution.
The Gordon Riots erupt in London.
The United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland is formed.
Nelson wins the Battle of Trafalgar and dies during the battle.
The Napoleonic Wars end after the Battle of Waterloo.
The Peterloo Massacre takes place in Manchester.
The Stockton and Darlington Railway opens, a milestone in railway transport.
The Great Reform Act expands parliamentary representation.
The Poor Law Amendment Act reforms poor relief.
Queen Victoria comes to the throne.
The Penny Black, the first adhesive postage stamp, is issued.
The Corn Laws are repealed after a major free trade campaign.
The Great Exhibition opens at the Crystal Palace in London.
The Metropolitan Railway opens in London, the world’s first underground railway.
The Second Reform Act expands the male electorate.
The Elementary Education Act lays the basis for wider state schooling.
The Third Reform Act extends voting rights to many rural men.
Queen Victoria dies and Edward VII becomes king.
The 1908 London Olympic Games open.
The Parliament Act limits the House of Lords’ power to block legislation.
Britain enters the First World War, affecting English towns, families and industry.
The Representation of the People Act gives votes to many women and all men over 21.
The General Strike brings much of the country to a halt.
Women gain voting rights on equal terms with men.
Edward VIII abdicates and George VI becomes king.
Britain declares war on Germany, bringing mobilisation, evacuation and rationing to England.
The Blitz causes major damage and loss of life in London and other English cities.
The National Health Service begins operating.
Elizabeth II becomes queen.
England wins the FIFA World Cup at Wembley Stadium.
The United Kingdom joins the European Economic Community.
Margaret Thatcher becomes the United Kingdom’s first female prime minister.
The Channel Tunnel opens between England and France.
The United Kingdom transfers Hong Kong to China and Tony Blair becomes prime minister.
Coordinated terrorist bombings strike London’s transport system.
The London 2012 Olympic Games open.
The United Kingdom votes to leave the European Union.
The United Kingdom leaves the European Union.
Elizabeth II dies and Charles III becomes king.
